home.title: Distributed Systems Architect & Protocol Designer
home.shortBio: Inventor of the Zenoh Protocol. Building the future of distributed computing — one protocol at a time.

about.heading: About
about.bio:
Angelo Corsaro is a globally recognized expert in distributed computing and the inventor of the Zenoh Protocol. With decades of experience designing high-performance middleware and communication protocols, he has shaped the way modern distributed systems communicate at scale.

His work spans real-time systems, edge computing, IoT, and robotics — domains where efficiency, scalability, and low latency are not optional but essential. Angelo is a frequent keynote speaker, open-source advocate, and thought leader in the distributed systems community.

zenoh.heading: Zenoh Protocol
zenoh.subtitle: Unifying the IoT / Edge / Cloud continuum
zenoh.tagline: A protocol that unifies data in motion, data at rest, and computations. From 2KB microcontrollers to cloud clusters.
zenoh.feature1Desc: Designed for the most resource-constrained environments. Zenoh's wire format is minimal, efficient, and deterministic.
zenoh.feature2Desc: From 2KB RAM microcontrollers to multi-datacenter cloud deployments. One protocol, every tier of your stack.
zenoh.feature3Desc: Producers and consumers are fully decoupled. Move resources between edge and cloud without changing application code.
zenoh.comingSoonTitle: Deep Dives Coming to the Blog
zenoh.comingSoonDesc: An in-depth technical series on Zenoh's design decisions, wire format, and real-world deployments is coming soon.
zenoh.repo1Desc: The Zenoh protocol implementation in Rust — the reference implementation.
zenoh.repo2Desc: A no_std Rust implementation of Zenoh for bare-metal and embedded targets.
zenoh.reportSubtitle: Monthly digest of Zenoh innovations, ecosystem highlights, and community stories.
zenoh.genesisSubtitle: How a protocol was born from necessity — and named after a philosopher
zenoh.bookSubtitle: The comprehensive guide to the Zenoh protocol — architecture, concepts, and hands-on examples.
zenoh.bookBuiltWith: Built with mdBook · Powered by Rust
zenoh.genesisIntro: Around 2009–2010, while serving as PrismTech’s CTO and co-chairing the OMG DDS Special Interest Group, Angelo Corsaro was working on some of the earliest Extremely Large Scale Systems — deployments spanning military, aerospace, and Smart City infrastructure. Two structural problems crystallised.
zenoh.genesisScaleProblemDesc: DDS could not scale up to global networks, nor down to constrained devices. CoAP was too cloud-centric; MQTT’s broker model forced even co-located devices to communicate through a server thousands of kilometres away.
zenoh.genesisTransparencyGapDesc: Pub/Sub had achieved location transparency for data in motion. But once data was stored, that transparency vanished — forcing everything onto centralised cloud storage. Applications needing low-latency access to stored data had no good answer.
zenoh.genesisFrankenstein: Systems of that era — what Angelo calls the “Digital Frankenstein” era — were assembled by duct-taping multiple protocol stacks together, crossing fingers for decent end-to-end semantics. His goal became clear: design a single protocol spanning microcontroller to data-centre, with no topological constraints and unified abstractions for data in motion, data at rest, and computations.
zenoh.genesisQuote: I had to do something. I started working on the idea of coming up with a protocol that could work efficiently from the microcontroller up to the data-center, would not have any topological constraints and would provide unified abstraction for dealing with data in movement and data at rest.
zenoh.genesisWhyText1: The name carries two philosophical references and one engineering acronym. Growing up in Sicily, Angelo was surrounded by the legacy of ancient Greek philosophy. He was particularly drawn to Zeno of Elea (Zenon d’Élée), whose paradoxes of infinity fascinated him from childhood.
zenoh.genesisWhyText2: Stoicism — founded by Zenon of Citium — left an equally deep mark through Marcus Aurelius’s Meditations.
zenoh.genesisWhyFootnote: The difference between Zenon and Zenoh is less than a letter — it is just the overshoot of the “h”.
